summary: Typosquat of chai-as-promised drops detached child that fetches and eval()s remote payload
details: Package name is a single-word edit of the popular chai-as-promised, and the README presents it as a chai plugin. When a consumer calls the exported middleware, index.js spawns `node lib/caller.js` as a detached child (`detached:true, stdio:'ignore'`, then `unref()`) so its activity is hidden from the parent. lib/caller.js disguises an attacker endpoint as a fake `process.env` shadow object containing base64-encoded fields (DEV_API_KEY, DEV_SECRET_KEY, DEV_SECRET_VALUE), which decode to `https://api.jsonstorage.net/v1/json/2ef8c758-a96f-459e-b036-b3b90379a165/a179ea35-b962-4722-b3f1-e28316d1a44a` and the `x-secret-key: _` header. The script GETs that JSON document, reads its `cookie` field, and executes the value via `new Function.constructor('require', s)(require)` — full Node RCE with `require` injected — retried up to 5 times. The hosting endpoint (api.jsonstorage.net) is anonymous, mutable, attacker-controlled storage; the executed bytes can change at any time without a package update. Typosquat naming, divergent API (advertised as a chai plugin while invoking a dropper), obfuscated C2 URL, detached evasion, and remote eval together leave no benign interpretation.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
